name: Black Pine Mine
short_description: BLM-managed historic mining area near Philipsburg offering free rockhounding for quartz, tourmaline, and feldspar specimens on gently sloping terrain.
description:
Black Pine Mine is a public BLM rockhounding site in Granite County, Montana, located near the historic mining town of Philipsburg. This area sits within the Northern Rocky Mountains where Cretaceous to early Tertiary granitic intrusions created extensive pegmatite and quartz-rich mineral deposits. The Black Pine Mine area represents classic Montana alpine pegmatite mining country, where tourmaline, quartz, and feldspar crystals formed in cooling granitic bodies. The site offers accessible rockhounding on gentle slopes with good visibility of exposed rock formations and weathered outcrops.

This location is managed by the Montana Bureau of Land Management and is open to personal-use collection under FLPMA regulations. Rockhounders can expect to find well-formed quartz specimens, tourmaline crystals (often black or greenish), feldspar fragments, and mica sheets in the weathered granite and pegmatite exposures. The gentle terrain makes it suitable for rockhounders of various experience levels, though the remote location requires preparation.

The area's mining history dates back to the late 1800s when prospectors worked the granite formations for precious minerals and gemstones. Historical diggings and exposed pegmatite veins remain visible today, making this an excellent site for understanding Montana's geological heritage while collecting modern specimens. The combination of historical significance and accessible geology makes Black Pine Mine a worthwhile destination for rockhounders visiting southwestern Montana.
history: The Black Pine Mine area sits within the Northern Rocky Mountains pegmatite belt, formed during Cretaceous to early Tertiary granitic intrusions when cooling magma created ideal conditions for large crystal formation. Historic mining activity dating to the late 1800s prospected these deposits for tourmaline and feldspar, leaving visible evidence of past work that helps modern rockhounders locate productive areas.
tips: Focus your search on weathered granite outcrops and exposed pegmatite veins on the hillsides; black tourmaline crystals often appear as dark linear features within white quartz. Look for flatter areas where rain has washed away lighter material, exposing darker tourmaline and feldspar fragments. Bring a small chisel or pry bar to work along pegmatite seams and quartz veins, and pack extra water since the elevation and remoteness limit access to services. The best specimens typically occur where previous digging has already exposed fresh rock faces.
terrain_notes: The site sits at approximately 6,000-6,500 feet elevation on gentle slopes typical of the Montana pegmatite district. Terrain is characterized by exposed granite and pegmatite outcrops with scattered whitebark pine and subalpine vegetation. Parking areas are accessed via rough dirt roads with moderate clearance requirements. Walking distance from parking to productive collecting areas is typically 0.25-1 mile across rolling terrain with firm footing and manageable grade.
best_season: June - September. High elevation and mountain climate mean heavy snow and difficult access October through May. Early summer (June-July) offers best conditions with dry roads and lower creek flows. Late summer through early September remains productive before fall storms.
written_directions: From Philipsburg, take MT 1 south for approximately 8 miles toward Anaconda. Turn east on Forest Road 173 (watch for BLM rockhounding area signs) and continue 3-4 miles on rough dirt road with moderate high-clearance vehicle recommended. Follow signs toward historic mining areas. GPS coordinates 46.447826, -113.366914 mark the primary collecting zone. High-clearance vehicle required; passenger cars may struggle with washboard and ruts, especially after rain.
road_conditions: Rough unimproved dirt road with washboard surface and potential ruts. High-clearance vehicle required. Road may be impassable during wet weather or heavy snow. No regular maintenance.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and turnarounds along access road near historic mining areas. No developed parking lot. Space for 3-5 vehicles at primary collecting zone. No facilities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. Pack out all trash. Respect historical mining features and do not disturb abandoned mine structures.
rules:

nearest_services: Philipsburg, approximately 10 miles northwest. Limited services - small grocery, gas, and restaurants. Anaconda, approximately 20 miles south, offers more complete services.
accessibility_notes: Gentle sloping terrain accessible to most hikers with moderate fitness. Uneven rocky footing and loose material common on hillsides. No wheelchair access due to rough road and uneven collecting areas. Sturdy hiking boots strongly recommended. High elevation (6,000+ ft) may cause fatigue for unacclimated visitors.

faq.question: What gems can I find rockhounding at Black Pine Mine in Montana?
faq.answer: Black Pine Mine is known for quartz specimens, black tourmaline crystals, feldspar fragments, and mica sheets. The site's pegmatite deposits produce well-formed quartz points and tourmaline crystals, making it excellent for collectors interested in Maine-style pegmatite minerals found in Montana's Northern Rockies.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Black Pine Mine?
faq.answer: No permit is required. Black Pine Mine is on BLM public land managed under FLPMA regulations. Rockhounding is free and open to the public for personal use collection without advance permission. You must follow the 25 lb per day collection limit and pack out all trash.

faq.question: How do I get to Black Pine Mine from Philipsburg?
faq.answer: From Philipsburg, take MT 1 south for 8 miles toward Anaconda, then turn east on Forest Road 173 and continue 3-4 miles on rough dirt road. High-clearance or AWD vehicles are recommended due to washboard ruts and potential wet weather challenges. GPS coordinates are 46.447826, -113.366914.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Black Pine Mine?
faq.answer: June through September is best. The high elevation (6,000+ ft) receives heavy snow October through May, making road access difficult or impossible. Early summer offers dry conditions and accessible roads, while late summer remains productive until fall storms arrive.

faq.question: Is Black Pine Mine suitable for beginner rockhounders?
faq.answer: Yes, Black Pine Mine is beginner-friendly with gentle slopes and exposed pegmatite outcrops that make finding specimens straightforward. Kids aged 8 and up can enjoy the site with supervision. The moderate terrain requires decent fitness for 0.25-1 mile walks, and the remote location demands preparation and self-sufficiency.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring to rockhound at Black Pine Mine?
faq.answer: Bring a small chisel or pry bar to work pegmatite seams, a rock hammer, magnifying glass, and collection bags. Most importantly, pack extra water since the remote location lacks services and elevation increases dehydration risk. Sturdy hiking boots, sun protection, and a headlamp are essential. A high-clearance vehicle is necessary for road access.

meta_description: Free rockhounding at Montana's Black Pine Mine. Find quartz, tourmaline, and feldspar on BLM public land near Philipsburg. June-September.
